question: What happens if no error occurs inside the try block?

options.text: The catch block runs with error equal to null
options.explanation: catch only executes when an error occurs, it is not called with null

options.text: The program throws an error because catch expects one
options.explanation: catch is an optional handler that is simply skipped when there is no error

options.text: The finally block will not execute
options.explanation: The finally block always executes — both on success and on error

question: Why is it bad to throw a string instead of an Error object?

options.text: JavaScript does not allow throwing strings
options.explanation: JavaScript allows throwing any value, including strings and numbers

options.text: It becomes impossible to determine where the error occurred
The string is automatically converted to an Error object
options.explanation: The string is not automatically converted — it remains a string in catch

question: Which properties does the Error object have?

options.explanation: The code property is not a standard Error property — it can be added in a custom class
The type property is not part of the standard Error object

question: What type does the error parameter have in a catch block in TypeScript?

options.explanation: TypeScript cannot guarantee that catch will receive an Error instance — it could be any value
Since TypeScript 4.0+, the catch parameter defaults to unknown, not any
A value of any type can end up in catch, not necessarily a string

question: What is wrong with an empty catch block?

options.explanation: An empty catch is valid JavaScript syntax

options.text: The error is completely hidden, making bugs invisible

options.explanation: An empty catch does not terminate the program — on the contrary, it lets it continue with invalid state
An empty catch does not affect the finally block — it will execute regardless
